Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: Training-Free Adaptive Precision Allocation via Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local spectral condition number and decay rate of hidden state activations serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free adaptive precision allocation. By computing these geometric diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, we can map them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods in training-free precision allocation and test-time optimization. However, our approach differs in that it combines the strengths of both SQR and SLS, using activation geometry to inform precision allocation and enforcing strict latency budgets through a precision-switching policy.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing (SPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local spectral diagnostics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, we map them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate SPR on WILDS benchmarks using a single-GPU setup
Compare SPR to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines in terms of throughput-accuracy tradeoff
Investigate the effect of different spectral diagnostics (e.g. condition number, decay rate) on precision allocation and latency
Analyze the theoretical bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of SPR may depend on the quality of the spectral diagnostics and the precision-switching policy
The computational overhead of computing spectral diagnostics on-the-fly may impact latency
The theoretical bounds may not hold in practice due to approximations or assumptions
